chief_complaint: A 75-year-old female patient presented with a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction.
history: Wellens syndrome is a condition equivalent to ST elevation myocardial infarction, highly specific for critical stenosis of the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) coronary artery. Prolonged QT interval is a risk factor for life-threatening arrhythmia, characterized by a prolongation of the QT interval (QTc) longer than 440 ms corrected for heart rhythm on the ECG. The patient had a normal sinus rhythm, negative T waves in leads I, aVL, and V6, and a QTc interval of 431 ms on admission.
exam: Vital signs and physical examination findings not provided.
labs: ECG: normal sinus rhythm, negative T waves in leads I, aVL, and V6, QTc interval of 431 ms; 2 days after coronary angiography: deep inverted T waves in the V2-V4 leads, QTc prolongation to 828 ms. Coronary angiography: critical stenosis of the distal left main (LM) and proximal LAD coronary artery.
correct_diagnosis: Wellens syndrome with prolonged QT interval in acute myocardial infarction due to LAD occlusion
differentials: Wellens syndrome with prolonged QT interval in acute myocardial infarction due to LAD occlusion
Acute myocardial infarction due to LAD occlusion
Prolonged QT interval in acute myocardial infarction
Critical stenosis of the proximal LAD coronary artery
correct_ranking: Wellens syndrome with prolonged QT interval in acute myocardial infarction due to LAD occlusion
Acute myocardial infarction due to LAD occlusion
Prolonged QT interval in acute myocardial infarction
Critical stenosis of the proximal LAD coronary artery
